study_notes.title: The Muscular System: Structure and Action
study_notes.introduction: Muscles are elastic tissues that generate force through contraction, allowing the body to move, circulate blood, and digest food. They work by pulling on bones through tendons.

key_properties.contraction: The process where muscle fibers shorten and thicken, becoming firm and exerting a pulling force.
key_properties.relaxation: The process where muscle fibers lengthen and soften, allowing the muscle to return to its resting state.
key_properties.antagonism: Because muscles can only PULL (and not push), they work in opposing pairs. When one contracts (shortens), the other must relax (lengthen) to allow movement.

voluntary_skeletal.characteristics: Striped (striated) appearance, consciously controlled by the brain.
voluntary_skeletal.function: Provides forceful, rapid movement for locomotion (walking, lifting).
voluntary_skeletal.example: Biceps (front of arm), Triceps (back of arm), Quadriceps (front of thigh).

involuntary_smooth.characteristics: Non-striped (smooth) appearance, works automatically without conscious effort.
involuntary_smooth.location: Walls of internal organs (stomach, intestines, bladder, blood vessels).
involuntary_smooth.function: Manages slow, sustained internal movements like digestion (peristalsis) and blood pressure regulation.

cardiac.characteristics: Striped but involuntary; specialized to pump blood continuously.
cardiac.location: Exclusively in the walls of the heart.
cardiac.function: Rhythmic, tireless contraction to circulate blood throughout the body.
cardiac.vital_fact: It never gets 'tired' (fatigued) like skeletal muscle because it is rich in mitochondria and oxygen.

biomechanics_of_antagonistic_pairs.definition: Pairs of muscles that work in opposition to move a joint.

example_1_the_elbow.flexion: Biceps contract (shorten/harden) to bend the elbow; Triceps relax.
example_1_the_elbow.extension: Triceps contract (shorten/harden) to straighten the arm; Biceps relax.

example_2_the_knee.flexion: Hamstrings contract to bend the knee.
example_2_the_knee.extension: Quadriceps contract to straighten the leg (e.g., kicking a ball).

essential_needs_for_muscles: Oxygen: Required to release energy from food.
Glucose: The primary fuel for muscle contraction.
Protein: The building block needed to repair muscle fibers after use.
Rest: Necessary for recovery from fatigue (lactic acid buildup).

muscle_fatigue.cause: Accumulation of lactic acid during intense physical activity when muscles don't get enough oxygen.
muscle_fatigue.symptoms: Pain, burning sensation, and inability to continue moving.

study_tips_for_ple: Cardiac muscle is the 'Specialist'—striped but automatic.
If you are lifting something, the flexor is the one that bulges and gets hard.
The 'P' in Patella makes it a 'Pulley' for the quadriceps, helping the leg straighten easier!
